title: String slices
instruction:
A string slice `&str` references part of a String. `&s[start..end]` creates it.

Run this.
successMessage: [..5] means from the beginning to index 4. [6..] means from index 6 to the end. No data is copied — just a view.

title: Array slices
instruction:
Slices work on any contiguous sequence. The type is `&[T]`.

Run this.
successMessage: &arr[1..4] creates a slice of 3 elements. The slice type here is &[i32]. It is a view, not a copy.

title: Functions accepting slices
instruction:
Use `&[T]` as the parameter type — it accepts any contiguous sequence.

Run this.
successMessage: sum accepts a slice so it works on both the whole array and any sub-range. &v coerces the array to a slice automatically.

title: Iterating a slice
instruction:
`for item in slice` iterates over references. Use `&` in the pattern to destructure.

Run this.
successMessage: for &p in &primes destructures the reference, giving you the value directly. The & in the for pattern and the & before primes both matter.

title: Mutable slices
instruction:
A mutable slice `&mut [T]` lets you modify elements in place.

Run this.
successMessage: Only indices 1, 2, 3 were scaled. *x dereferences the mutable reference to write through it.

title: Slice methods
instruction:
Slices have useful built-in methods.

Run this.
successMessage: sort modifies in place. first and last return Option because the slice might be empty. contains checks membership.

title: Splitting slices
instruction:
`split_at(n)` divides a slice into two at index n.

Run this.
successMessage: split_at returns two non-overlapping slices. No data is copied — both are views into the original array.

title: windows and chunks
instruction:
`windows(n)` gives overlapping windows; `chunks(n)` gives non-overlapping groups.

Run this.
successMessage: windows slides over the data with overlap. chunks divides without overlap. Both are zero-copy views.
